sentences.text: Clara, C-L-A-R-A.
Bronstein, B-R-O-N-S-T-E-I-N.
Zawodnik, Cheyah.
Z-A-W-O-D-N-I-K.
Date?
June 22nd, 1924.
I was born in Miedzyrzecz, Poland.
M-E-M-I-E-N-D-Z-Y-C.
I think.
Well, it was a small town.
It bordered on the Russian border.
And the population as I can sort of remember was about 5,000 Jews.
But there were also Poles there and Ukrainians.
But I can't really recall the full amount how many people were there.
Well, yes.
It was like the center of the city.
So it was Jewish, more or less.
But there were a lot of other nationalities there, too.
Yes, I had a mother and father.
My mother's name was Nechama and my father's name was Israel.
And I had two brothers and a sister.
And their names were Aron was the oldest brother.
And Gershon was the second.
And my sister's name was Bayla.
I was the oldest.
Well, as I said, it was a small town.
It was a very close, neat Jewish town.
People-- there were synagogues.
There was a Hebrew school.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] My parents were I would say Orthodox in those days.
They kept a kosher home.
And we were the children.
We were all sent to Hebrew school.
It was called the talbot.
And, [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] well, it was a nice life, I would say.
In Poland during those days when between the wars was quite liberal.
It was a democracy.
So we, although there was anti-Semitism, but we didn't feel it.
Because, first of all, we didn't mixed a lot with the Gentile people.
And secondly, I think it wasn't too bad in the beginning.
But then in the later years around '37, '38, when the Germans started with their propaganda, it reflect on our hearts, too.
And things started to be a little tougher.
Yes.
We had actually a very nice house.
It was like you would call it here a duplex, two houses together.
It was my dad and my uncle.
And there were two children in their family.
And we were almost always together having lots of fun.
My parents were in a food business.
Export business.
Because the country where I came from grows a lot of food.
And they used to lease orchards from-- well, what do you call it?
The [NON-ENGLISH].
I don't know how to say.
The [NON-ENGLISH]?
The [NON-ENGLISH].
And they would harvest the food and they'd bring it into town to [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] storages.
And they would pack it and they'd ship it.
Even to Canada they used to ship it in those days.
Also, we had horses because of the business.
And when we were children they used to take us to those orchards sometime, like on holidays.
And we had lots of fun.
We were very good, nice, good family.
Well, they had barns.
Our house sort of bordered on a creek and a big-- and it was like here you would call it acreage.
So the horse weren't kept with us.
But, I mean, just to describe the house.
So my dad and my uncle decided one day that they want to have very fresh fish for themselves.
So they dug out-- not they, but somebody dug out-- a pond.
And they let fish in, they brought fish.
And they used to go and catch the fish.
And we children used to go and-- of course, we had friends and everything.
And further in the river there, or the creek-- it wasn't that big, the river.
We used to go swimming.
And it was nice.
Well, in those days, by those standards, I think we were pretty good, pretty well off.
At least, I didn't remember lacking too much in anything.
[LAUGHS]
No, it didn't.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] You know, it was a family business.
It was passed down from generations.
And I don't think that they had business-- like, it wasn't an open store that you called and it had a name.
But it was just the business there.
My mother was just at home with the children.
Yes.
She was involved in just about everything we did.
And we were involved in all kinds of things as children.
We were involved in organizations, in Zionist organizations.
And my parents were very much-- I think it was the era where the Zionists flourished in Poland.
And just about everybody was somebody.
And the focus mainly wasn't actually going to Israel.
People, young people-- well, I was yet a little young.
But older in their 20s they were preparing to go to Israel.
They took on-- [PAUSES FOR 7 SECONDS] they were learning not professions, but what other thing.
Work, like--
Maybe.
That's right.
And so it was a flourishing place.
Yes.
It was-- well, the subject-- of course, it was Poland, so you had to take Polish as your language and everything.
But we took everything in two languages.
In Hebrew-- not just Hebrew as a language or-- but it was math in Hebrew, history, everything, everything, in Hebrew.
And the same thing in Polish.
And also we had to take two other languages like two foreign languages.
There was a choice.
You could take either English or German.
I preferred to take German.
I don't know why.
I wish it was that I took English.
So happened that I took German.
And we also had to take Latin.
And the school was very hamlish.
You know, like the parents did everything.
We used to put on plays every holiday.
We used to go on [NON-ENGLISH].
Yes.
And it was very pleasant.
Yes.
Yes, I used to be.
We used to even as kids we used to walk around and speak Hebrew.
Because our mother tongue was Yiddish.
We spoke Yiddish at home.
But we were showing off and we used to speak Hebrew like amongst the friends.
And then in the organizations.
I belonged to Hashomer Hatzair.
And there everything was conducted in Hebrew.
We sang, we danced, we had lovely times.
Well, I sort remember some, but I can't recall exactly.
But I know maybe to the bigger cities.
Like our bigger city from [NON-ENGLISH SPEECH] was Rivne.
And there was a lot more people.
And I think that they send them there.
And from there it got around to the smaller towns whatever they had to say.
But we were ardent Zionists, I can tell you that much.
Myself?
No, I was too young.
I was in the beginning of the--
Well, certainly-- well, of course there were the government, the post office.
I mean, things like that you had-- you had to-- to relate to them.
But I think everything else was almost Jewish.
All businesses, all whatever there was in town  it was Jewish.
Whatever you needed.
When we grew a little older and-- OK.
I'll tell you when we did have.
When we used to skate.
I mean, they skated, too.
So we were sort of running into each other.
Or boys when they went playing football and they went in, you know, things like that.
But not too much.
Not very much, I must say.
Incidents?
Personally, I didn't experience any.
But you heard things like in universities they wouldn't let too many.
There was a quota.
And the Jews had to sit in the back of the room.
They couldn't, you know.
But when I went to-- in our town there were only seven grades of schooling.
So when you wanted a little higher education you had to go to a bigger city, which was Rivne.
And they had a talbot gymnasium there.
Yes, I attended there.
Two years only.
And then the war broke out.
And-- [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] what was I going to say?
Yeah.
So, you know, I was always in Jewish circles.
And I really didn't experience that much.
Yes.
Right.
He-- not from the Polish.
But we had a lot of Ukrainians around us.
I would say that they had at times, like in the season, 50 people working for them.
And most of them were Ukrainian.
They used to be quite nice to us.
We had good relationships.
And let's say in the home like to wash clothes.
They didn't have washing machines in those days.
So we used to get two Ukrainian women.
Used to come into the house for a week, and wash clothes, and do things like that, do your house cleaning.
But that's about, I think, the extent that we dealt with the Ukrainians.
Well, I don't think in those days there were too many goals that women had.
And I don't think I aspired to anything.
But my mother, I would say, she was very much behind-- not too many girls went to higher education.
You finished the seventh grade and that was it.
But my mother really was-- you know, she had high hopes, I guess.
And she sent us to learn more and to be better.
But I don't think that it was meant to be as an occupation or as a goal.
It was just to be better.
To, you know, better yourself.
And so I wouldn't say that I have anything in mind especially.
But I really loved Zionism.
And used to think that if I get older, I would like to live in Palestine.
This was one of my aspirations.
Well, in '39 Poland was divided in half.
Our occupation was by the Soviets.
And as soon as they marched in with their armies, they confiscated all businesses.
Anything they could lay their hands on, so to speak.
And life, of course, changed right away.
But not that much for the worse.
Because as children, young people, we were young, and we sort of welcomed the Russian army.
Because they-- they showed might.
I mean, maybe it wasn't there, but that's the way it looked to us.
And what-- what we thought, what we knew already what Germany was.
That was more or less for the better that they came in.
But life was a little harsher.
Everybody had to-- then you had to work somehow.
I still was at an age where I could go to school.
In a few months, they would open the schools and I went back to school.
And they were teaching us Russian, of course.
And-- but it wasn't too bad.
And--
Yes, it did.
It did.
They took away everything like they had.
But my father was a veteran from the First World War.
Although in Poland he wasn't recognized because he was from the-- that part of Poland used to be Russia, under the Tzar.
So he was actually in the Russian army.
But he was a veteran.
And they somehow-- the Soviets did recognize him.
And they gave him a little liquor business that he could operate.
And that helped us a lot.
Because at least, you know, we had something.
Otherwise, lots of people are very, very poor.
And a lot of people were sent to Siberia.
So we were on that better off.
Yes.
Well, some people they-- who they thought were wealthy, rich people they sent them to Siberia.
Some of them.
Not a whole family.
Let's say, from a family the father had to go, because he was the-- the rich guy.
Well, first of all, when the war broke out and the occupied Poland, the western end of Poland, like Warsaw, all those big cities, a lot of the refugees came streaming into our part of the world.
So-- well, only Jews, not anybody else.
So we took them all in and they lived with us.
Some, I don't know.
Three months, a half of year.
And then the Russians send them out to Siberia.
But we had a pretty good idea what was going on there.
They told us.
But, you know, we really didn't believe it.
Never thought it can happen.
And [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] either than that, I don't think that there were too many newspapers in those days that you could really get any information.
Before the war, we had.
We had a Jewish newspaper in our town.
We had the Polish newspaper.
And we knew, you know.
Things filtered through.
But during the Russian occupation, we didn't hear that much.
But those refugees, they brought all the good news with them.
Yes.
As a matter of fact, I remember my uncle-- he was the oldest in the family.
He said-- because a lot of Jews when the war broke out went back to-- they retreated to Russia because that-- and those people, most of them, were alive to save themself.
But my uncle said, we are not moving.
None of us is going anywhere.
We're not going to leave here.
We're staying right here.
The Germans can't be all that bad.
He remembered them from before the First World War.
And, of course, then it was a different story.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]
So we stayed.
And nobody survived.
I was the only one survivor that was left, from the whole family.
What happened next?
Well, next came the war.
The '41, I think.
And the Germans broke the pact with the Russians, with the Soviets.
And they marched.
They came into Poland.
And they came in with such might, with such strength, with such [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] fierce [? and this ?] hate, I think it was.
They bombed us.
Even the small town.
Like, ours was a small town.
We didn't have nothing there.
They used to come down with their planes so low that they used to shoot on the people.
Whoever happens to be where they were, then they got killed.
And it was a terrible panic, the first few weeks.
But then things settled a bit.
The army passed.
I mean, they went further.
And behind the Russian army always came an SS command.
Sorry.
The German army.
Came the SS command.
They were the ones that dealt with the population, with the civilian population.
So, of course, they [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] destroyed just about everything.
Yes.
Very much.
You were scared to walk outside.
You was-- you didn't have any food.
You were really, really suffering.
They made us feel like the lowest-- I don't know that-- you know, worse than-- and you know, you felt it.
I don't know why.
On me it made a very terrible impression.
And they started out saying that all Juden have to go to work.
They organized brigades, I guess they call them.
Everybody-- young, old, sick, whatever.
You had to go to work.
They used to take us out on [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] sort of the main-- [PAUSES FOR 8 SECONDS] I just-- Like the main--
Well, not the main street.
They called it the plaza.
They used to-- everybody used to had to get out there.
Besides the Germans, beside the SS, the Ukrainians were-- even I think, myself-- they were worse.
They gave them a free hand.
And, of course, the Ukrainians knew everybody.
So they-- you couldn't-- you couldn't hide yourself.
You couldn't get away.
They were right there.
Kicking, shoving, some killing.
Some terrible things happened.
And so in the first few months, they made us all go to work.
We went.
Wherever they sent us, of course, we went.
Oh, yes.
We used to go-- they used to send us to [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] first of all to clean up the city.
They made a big mess and then they send us to clean up.
Even mundane work as cleaning windows they made us do.
They didn't-- we're not going to have like the business section.
They weren't going to put a business there because it used to be all Jewish and, of course, it wasn't there anymore.
But they still out of spite they made us do terrible things.
But and then they decided that all Jews would move into a ghetto.
So from the whole town, everybody, they gave us one, big street.
And that's where everybody moved in.
Yeah.
Everybody was allowed just one room.
So if you had five rooms in your house, you had have five families in your house.
But, of course--
No, we didn't.
We were living on that street where the Jewish--
Where the ghetto was established.
They also established a Judenrat, which meant it was the liaison between the Germans and the Jews.
So whatever they wanted, they gave it to them.
Any they--
Yes.
You bet.
I knew them all.
And the Judenrat had to supply, well, money or anything else.
We didn't have anymore.
They took it all away.
So people to work.
Young people, men, women too.
And they sent them out to different places to work.
Whatever work they needed.
The roads, the-- everything.
I had an uncle there that was a fur maker.
And he had this-- and they needed warm clothes.
So they let him keep his business and keep all the people working for him to produce warm hats, gloves, coats, everything.
So our family mostly went to work for this uncle.
So we were a little bit maybe better off than most in this way that we didn't suffer so much.
We didn't-- you know, weren't so run around by the Germans.
We sort of had a place to go to work.
We are out of the--
